They understand the balance of words and pictures on a page or on a screen. Why is this important? This is important for a simple but important reason that seems to be discounted by anyone who has ever built a deck in the history of the universe. Human cognition. Let's think about how humans process information for one moment. Have you ever built a deck or been subjected to a deck where someone is speaking to you? And on the screen, there is dozens or hundreds of words or a complicated schematic that shows the inner workings of an organization or the development of a product. Have you ever been spoken to in a way that you're trying to process the information on the screen while listening to the person speak because that is almost every deck I have ever seen in my entire life, which is to say, it's impossible to understand. Here's how human beings work. We can either listen to what is being said and ignore the visual stimuli when it is overwhelming or we can ignore what is being said and decode the visual stimuli when it is overwhelming or we can do what most people do, which is they try to do both, which is the worst thing in the world because that means they don't hear or see anything. They struggle to capture the visual cues and the visual information provided while simultaneously listening to someone speak about it. And what happens in those moments is comprehension collapses. And when comprehension collapses, people stop listening because you've no longer given them something to listen to. You've no longer given them a reason to pay attention to you. You must ask yourself at all times when you're building a deck, am I considering the process of human comprehension? Could someone possibly look at this slide and simultaneously listen to me and understand, decode, comprehend, and absorb both simultaneously? It is possible. Steve Jobs understood it perfectly. That's why all of his slides had four words, six words, eight words, mostly visual, only supporting what he was saying when necessary. He understood well that people cannot process large amounts of information visually and auditorily, simultaneously, and yet 99 percent of the decks I have ever seen in my life attempt to demand that from human beings. Let's stop doing that now. Let's create performances supported by visuals that work in concert with each other so that the visuals enhance comprehension. They support and promote understanding. While I'm speaking, so I need to present it one small element at a time. I also understand that in order to engage people, to get them to listen, to pay attention, to want to hear the next thing I'm going to say, I have to create things like mental schemas, buckets that need to be filled. I need to provide transitions so that one slide transitions to the other side that transitions to the other side. So they feel attached. So they're not just six slides that you could shuffle around in any order. You can't shuffle my presentation around in any order because every single slide is predicated on the previous slide and dependent for the next slide. That is what a story is. How many times have I been looking at a slide deck and someone just shuffles the order of the slides because they prefer to say it in this order? As soon as you can shuffle the order, listen carefully, as soon as you can shuffle the order of slides in your deck, you're no longer telling the story. You're doing a book report. Whenever you can just slide one slide, three slides earlier, you're no longer telling a story. Stories are scenes that are predicated on the previous scene and cause the next scene to happen. Slides must be ordered in such a way that they actually tell a story that if you take one slide out, it doesn't make sense anymore. That is how you build a deck. Each one of my slides leads to the next slide. There's no question in my mind what is coming because I know it is coming because it only makes sense what's coming because I just said something that has determined what the next thing will be. How do we close a presentation like this? I think this is the best way. We do it in a personal way and we bring it back full circle. Do you remember how we opened this? I ended my presentation with pictures of my actual dog, Kaylee, who passed away six years ago, a dog I lived with for 18 years. And in that corporate room that day, people cried. I was Matthew Dix, dog owner, dog lover, who introduced them at the end to Kaylee, my favorite friend who is no longer with me. Now, when they cried at the end, if they had been customers, do you think I would have had any trouble getting them to buy my dog shoes? I wouldn't. And it wasn't manipulative. It was authentic. It was real. It is genuinely how I felt. I thought to myself when I saw that product, did my dog have something like that? And if not, why not? I infused myself into the presentation. The same way Steve Jobs would infuse himself into all of his presentations. When you build a deck, are you building a deck for anyone? Or are you building it for you? If you're building your deck in such a way that anyone can deliver it, then no one should be delivering it because it will be meaningless to the audience. At some point in the midst of your performance, you need to become a part of the performance. It doesn't need to be a big ending moment like I have presented here. It can be something simple at the beginning that indicates who you are. It indicates what you believe in. It can be as simple as talking about your kids for 12 seconds. It can be as simple as talking about how you love playing basketball and you think that dandelions are actually an oddly beautiful on the front lawn. That makes you a human being. You find a way to put yourself into the performance too. Even when you're talking about something as seemingly pedestrian as dog choose, you have to be part of the performance too. You have to be part of the talk track. You have to be part of the visuals that support the talking that you're doing. That is how we build the deck. That is how we capture the minds and hearts of our audience. And that's how we get people to continue to listen to us as we speak. And so in summary, read picture books. Sincerely, read picture books. They will teach you almost everything you need to know. Take stock in human comprehension and understand that people cannot and will not process visual information and auditory information simultaneously when they are overwhelmed and that the human brain has only so much bandwidth to process both. Ask yourself as you're building your deck. Can people even pay attention to what I'm putting out there? Separate your content into more than one slide whenever possible. Present smaller amounts of information on more slides so that people can understand what you're saying while they're looking at your slides. Put fewer words on all of your slides. Say a lot of things, but only use the sentences that you want to punch audiences with on the slides themselves. Build slides without any words at all. Use slides for humor. Create site gags from your slides. Build slides with humor because humor always works. When my mother passed away the next day, I was sitting across from a funeral director and he made me laugh and I was grateful for it. Humor always belongs in every performance. Have multiple clicks on a slide. Have multiple clicks on a slide. Don't be afraid to present small bits of information on a slide. One bit of information at a time. Build your slides so that they create mental buckets in your audience's head, create schemas that audiences must fill. Be sure that one slide leads to the next slide in a meaningful and purposeful way. Remember, if you can shuffle your slides around, you're no longer telling a story. You're simply providing a book report. And put yourself into your presentation. Don't create presentations that anyone off the street can present. Make sure it's only you who can perform what you're going to say and what you're going to show by putting a little bit of yourself into what you have to say. I have a million things I can tell you about building a deck, but this is a great start. This is what you need to truly transform what you're doing and to make a real difference in what you're doing. These are the strategies that are going to help you become more comprehensible, more understandable, more memorable and most important. These are the strategies that are going to get people to want to hear what you have to say. These are the strategies that are going to hold audiences attention, engage them and ideally sell more stuff. Get more people on board. Get more people believing in you.
